Battle.brief: A class that encapsulates the parameters and state management for a battle scenario within the game.
Battle.details: The Battle class models a combat encounter or arena challenge, managing key parameters such as the level boundaries (\u201clow\u201d and \u201chigh\u201d) to determine eligible participants, and a \u201cfee\u201d that represents the cost associated with entering or participating in the battle. It maintains internal state flags, \u201cissued\u201d to indicate whether a challenge has been issued, and \u201cstart\u201d to denote if the battle has begun. The class provides a default constructor for initializing these parameters to default values and a virtual destructor to ensure proper cleanup in inheritance hierarchies. Copy constructor and assignment operator are declared private to prevent unintended copying, maintaining control over battle instances. This class serves as a foundational component for managing battle logic, ensuring consistent state management and parameter configuration across the game system.
Battle.notes: The class employs the singleton-like pattern for copy control by declaring the copy constructor and assignment operator private, preventing copying of battle instances. This design ensures that each battle instance is uniquely managed, avoiding potential conflicts or inconsistent states.
Battle.rationale: The Battle class exists to provide a structured way to configure and track the state of individual battles, facilitating game balance, challenge management, and resource handling. It integrates into the larger system by serving as a core component for initiating, monitoring, and controlling combat scenarios, ensuring that game rules related to level eligibility, entry fees, and challenge states are consistently enforced.
Battle.name: Battle

low.brief: Stores the lower boundary of the battle level range used to determine combat eligibility.
low.details: The 'low' variable is initialized to zero and represents the minimum level threshold for battles within the game. It is set dynamically based on user input or game conditions and is utilized in functions that check whether characters fall within the permissible battle level range. This variable interacts with other level boundary variables to define the overall combat level window, influencing both engagement rules and status displays during gameplay.
low.notes: Typically stored as an integer, 'low' is often updated during battle setup or configuration phases to reflect the desired minimum level for combat participation.
low.rationale: This variable exists to establish and enforce the lower limit of the battle level range, ensuring that only characters within the specified levels can engage in combat, thereby maintaining game balance and fairness.
low.name: low

usages.fight_8cc, void do_battle(Character *ch,: Stores the lower level boundary for the battle range, set from user input or used in status display.",

high.brief: Stores the upper boundary of the battle level range used in combat calculations.
high.details: The 'high' variable defines the maximum level boundary for a battle, determining which characters or entities are included in combat scenarios based on their levels. It is initialized to zero and is typically set dynamically during battle setup, often based on user input or game state conditions. This value influences calculations related to damage, experience, and other level-dependent mechanics within the battle system.
high.notes: As an integer, 'high' is stored efficiently and updated as needed to reflect the current upper level limit for ongoing or upcoming battles.
high.rationale: This variable exists to facilitate flexible and dynamic management of battle level ranges, ensuring that combat calculations accurately consider only the relevant characters within the specified level boundaries.
high.name: high

usages.fight_8cc, void do_battle(Character *ch,: Stores the upper level boundary for the battle range, set from user input or used in status display.",

fee.brief: An integer representing the fee associated with entering or participating in a battle or arena.
fee.details: The 'fee' variable within the Battle class indicates the cost or charge required for a character to participate in a battle or arena event. It is initialized to zero and can be set dynamically based on game conditions or player input. This variable is utilized in functions such as 'kill_off' to deduct the fee after a character's kill, and in 'do_battle' to determine or display the entry fee for participants. Proper management of this variable ensures accurate resource tracking and enforces game mechanics related to costs and penalties.
fee.notes: The 'fee' is typically stored as a simple integer and may be reset or updated as needed when game events occur. Its consistent use across related functions helps maintain clear and predictable fee handling.
fee.rationale: The 'fee' variable exists to facilitate the management of costs associated with battle participation, enabling the game to enforce entry charges, penalties, or resource deductions, thereby supporting balanced gameplay and resource economy.
fee.name: fee

usages.fight_8cc, void do_battle(Character *ch,: Stores the fee amount for entering the battle, set from user input or used in status display.",
usages.fight_8cc, void kill_off(Character *ch,: Referenced when deducting costs in arena sectors after a kill, possibly representing a fee amount.

issued.brief: A boolean flag indicating whether a battle or recall challenge has been issued for a character.
issued.details: The 'issued' variable serves as a status indicator that tracks whether a battle or recall challenge has been initiated for a specific character in the game. It is initialized to false upon character creation or reset, and is set to true when a challenge is issued through relevant commands. This flag is checked in functions like 'do_battle' and 'recall' to control command flow, prevent duplicate challenges, and manage game state transitions. Its proper management ensures consistent behavior during combat or recall operations.
issued.notes: The variable is typically reset to false after the challenge is resolved or canceled, maintaining accurate status tracking throughout gameplay.
issued.rationale: The 'issued' flag exists to coordinate challenge states, ensuring that characters do not issue multiple simultaneous challenges and that game logic responds appropriately to ongoing challenges.
issued.name: issued

usages.act__move_8cc, void recall(Character *ch: Variable used to determine if a recall command has been issued, affecting certain conditional logic.
usages.fight_8cc, void do_battle(Character *ch,: Indicates whether a battle challenge has been issued, used to control command flow and status responses.",

start.brief: A boolean flag indicating whether a battle has been initiated.
start.details: The `start` variable within the `Battle` class signifies whether a battle has officially begun. It is initialized to `false`, indicating that no battle is in progress at the outset. This flag is checked across various functions to control game mechanics such as attack permissions, arena safety, and timing-related features. When set to `true`, it signals that combat has commenced, enabling relevant behaviors and restrictions throughout the battle lifecycle.
start.notes: The variable is primarily used as a state indicator and is typically toggled at the start and end of a battle sequence. Its boolean nature allows for straightforward condition checks to manage game flow.
start.rationale: The `start` flag exists to coordinate game logic around the initiation of battles, ensuring that certain actions are only permitted once a battle has officially started, thereby maintaining consistent game rules and flow.
start.name: start

Battle::Battle().brief: Initializes a new Battle instance with default settings.
Battle::Battle().details: The Battle constructor creates a new instance of the Battle class, establishing the initial state necessary for a battle. Although currently empty, it relies on default member initializations to set up the internal data structures. This constructor prepares the object for subsequent configuration and execution of battle logic, ensuring that the instance starts in a valid and consistent state before any further operations are performed.
Battle::Battle().returns: This constructor does not return a value.
Battle::Battle().notes: This constructor is designed for use at the beginning of a battle scenario, serving as the foundational setup point before additional parameters or configurations are applied.
Battle::Battle().rationale: Used when creating a new battle instance to ensure a clean, default-initialized state before setting up specific battle conditions or starting the simulation.
Battle::Battle().name: Battle

virtual Battle::~Battle().brief: Virtual destructor for the Battle class to ensure proper cleanup of resources in inheritance hierarchies.
virtual Battle::~Battle().details: This destructor is declared as virtual to guarantee that the correct destructor is invoked for objects of derived classes when deleted through a pointer to the base Battle class. Although the destructor body is empty, declaring it virtual is crucial for proper resource management and cleanup in inheritance hierarchies, especially if derived classes allocate resources that need explicit release. This design ensures that all resources are properly released, preventing memory leaks or resource dangling issues when deleting derived class instances via base class pointers.
virtual Battle::~Battle().returns: This function does not return a value.
virtual Battle::~Battle().notes: Having a virtual destructor is essential in polymorphic base classes to ensure that destructors of derived classes are called correctly during object destruction.
virtual Battle::~Battle().rationale: This destructor is used when deleting objects through base class pointers, ensuring that all derived class cleanup code executes properly, which is vital for resource management in complex inheritance scenarios.
virtual Battle::~Battle().name: ~Battle

Battle::Battle(const Battle &).brief: Copy constructor for the Battle class.
Battle::Battle(const Battle &).details: Creates a new instance of the Battle class as a copy of an existing Battle object. This constructor duplicates the state of the provided Battle object, ensuring that the new instance is a separate but identical copy.
Battle::Battle(const Battle &).name: Battle

Battle & Battle::operator=(const Battle.brief: Assigns the values from another Battle object to this one.
Battle & Battle::operator=(const Battle.details: This assignment operator copies all the data members from the provided Battle object to the current object, effectively making them identical. It ensures that any resources are properly managed and that the current object is left in a valid state after the assignment.

params.other: The Battle object whose values are to be copied.
